summary: Today's sweep leans toward Democracy / press freedom, AI / privacy, Democracy / anti-fascism. The strongest items in this run are Live updates from Elon Musk and Sam Altman’s court battle over the future of OpenAI, Israel says it will sue New York Times over article on sexual abuse of Palestinian prisoners, and US border patrol chief resigns abruptly amid string of exits by Trump immigration officials.

mainItems.title: Live updates from Elon Musk and Sam Altman’s court battle over the future of OpenAI
mainItems.summary: Sam Altman and Elon Musk are facing off in a high-stakes trial that could alter the future of OpenAI and its most well-known product, ChatGPT.
mainItems.whyItMatters: Touches trust, surveillance, and the private use of AI systems.

mainItems.title: Israel says it will sue New York Times over article on sexual abuse of Palestinian prisoners
mainItems.summary: Media law experts cast doubt on viability of a defamation lawsuit promised by Netanyahu over Nicholas Kristof essay Israel ’s prime minister, Benjamin Nethanyahu , and foreign minister, Gideon Sa’ar, have threatened to sue the New York…
mainItems.whyItMatters: Touches trust, surveillance, and the private use of AI systems.

mainItems.title: US border patrol chief resigns abruptly amid string of exits by Trump immigration officials
mainItems.summary: Mike Banks, who led Trump’s border crackdown, resigned weeks after reports of prostitution allegations Sign up for the Breaking News US newsletter email Mike Banks, the border patrol chief who oversaw the most aggressive militarization of…
mainItems.whyItMatters: Worth watching for authoritarian drift, selective enforcement, and democratic norms.

mainItems.title: Razer’s Blade 18 is getting a $500 price hike and a new Intel chip
mainItems.summary: Razer just announced a new Blade 18 gaming laptop with an Intel Core Ultra 9 290HX Plus chip. The flagship laptop is available now on Razer's site, starting at $3,999.99 with an Nvidia RTX 5070 Ti and configurable with up to an RTX 5090.
mainItems.whyItMatters: Relevant to state power, civil liberties, and anti-authoritarian concerns.

mainItems.title: Opinion: Report Finds Books Aren’t Vanishing From Schools. But That’s Not the Whole Story
mainItems.summary: A version of this essay originally appeared on “The Next 30 Years” Substack. A new Rand report on whole-book reading in secondary English classrooms arrives at a useful moment.
mainItems.whyItMatters: Directly relevant to teaching, classroom practice, and educational judgment.

mainItems.summary: Transcripts reveal billionaire Ted Waitt’s relationship with Epstein accomplice and $7.

mainItems.summary: Subpoenas demanded birthdates, social security numbers and addresses of patients who got gender-affirming care Sign up for the Breaking News US newsletter email A federal judge has blocked the Trump administration ’s sweeping demands for…

mainItems.title: How companies weaponize the terms of service against you
mainItems.summary: Today, I’m talking with Brendan Ballou, founder of the Public Integrity Project and author of a new book, called When Companies Run the Courts, about the rise of forced arbitration.
mainItems.whyItMatters: Relevant to state power, civil liberties, and anti-authoritarian concerns.

mainItems.title: Establishing AI and data sovereignty in the age of autonomous systems
mainItems.summary: When generative AI first moved from research labs into real-world business applications, enterprises made a tacit bargain: “Capability now, control later.
mainItems.whyItMatters: Relevant to the shape of current AI systems and where the tooling is moving.

mainItems.title: AI invades Princeton, where 30% of students cheat—but peers won't snitch
mainItems.summary: Old "honor code" systems are under strain.
mainItems.whyItMatters: Directly relevant to teaching, student agency, and school policy.

mainItems.title: DOGE Cuts Unleashed a Deadly Wave of Violence Across Africa, Study Finds
mainItems.summary: The dismantling of the United States Agency for International Development (USAID) is associated with measurable increases in Africa, especially in areas most dependent on the agency’s support.

mainItems.title: A brain-controlled system may help listeners with hearing loss cut through the noise
mainItems.summary: A hearing system that monitors brain waves could help people with hearing loss communicate in noisy environments.
mainItems.whyItMatters: Relevant to your current interest graph.

mainItems.title: The physics of how Olympic weightlifters exploit barbell's "whip"
mainItems.summary: The type of bar matters when it comes to how it bends and recoils, but why is still a mystery.
mainItems.whyItMatters: Relevant to systems, infrastructure, and technical risk.

new.title: Scavengers Reign
new.why: Animated speculative sci-fi with strong worldbuilding and a weirder imagination than most prestige SF.

new.title: Sugar
new.why: Noir detective structure with a genre wrinkle and a sensibility that feels adjacent to your crime/strangeness overlap.

new.title: The Lazarus Project
new.why: Time-loop espionage, moral pressure, and speculative plotting — very plausible fit.

new.title: Bodies
new.why: Time-spanning conspiracy/mystery structure that fits your taste for long-arc puzzle storytelling.

new.title: Constellation
new.why: A little more cerebral and mood-heavy, but it scratches the identity / reality-slippage itch.

new.title: The Devil’s Hour
new.why: Dark, twisty, and structurally ambitious without feeling like homework.

older.title: Counterpart
older.why: Probably the single cleanest “James show you may have missed”: espionage + parallel-world sci-fi + adult competence.

older.title: Patriot
older.why: Dry, strange, melancholy spy storytelling with a very distinct voice.

older.title: Utopia
older.why: Paranoid conspiracy energy, formal boldness, and a willingness to get strange and ugly.

older.title: Rubicon
older.why: A low-key conspiracy thriller that leans heavily on intelligence work, paranoia, and institutional atmosphere.

older.title: Continuum
older.why: A cleaner old-school sci-fi fit if you want procedural momentum wrapped around time-travel politics.

older.title: Person of Interest
older.why: Starts procedural and then quietly becomes one of the smartest AI / surveillance shows on television.

shared.title: Bad Sisters
shared.why: Darkly funny, well-observed, and ensemble-driven in a way that plausibly overlaps with Hacks / Derry Girls / Mare of Easttown energy.

shared.title: Detectorists
shared.why: Gentle, funny, humane, and specific — a softer shared-watch possibility.

shared.title: Astrid et Raphaëlle
shared.why: French procedural with a strong central duo and exactly the kind of foreign-crime appeal Maggie may share with you.

shared.title: Somebody Somewhere
shared.why: A warmer character-driven recommendation if the shared overlap leans more Bear / Good Place than pure detective work.

highlights.title: 12 unexpected sourdough bread recipes that aren't crusty loaves: From sourdough flatbread to sourdough milk bread — and even sourdough babka.
highlights.notes: Pinned from today's briefing, the kind of bread-nerd rabbit hole worth keeping.
